ncs_standard_definition.description_short: An Architectural Critic analyzes and evaluates the design, functionality, and aesthetics of buildings and urban spaces, providing expert opinions and insights to architects, developers, and the public.
ncs_standard_definition.description_detailed: An Architectural Critic conducts in-depth research, site visits, and interviews to assess the architectural and urban design of buildings, public spaces, and infrastructure projects. They analyze the design's impact on the environment, community, and society, and provide constructive feedback to architects, developers, and policymakers to improve the built environment.
ncs_standard_definition.official_min_education: Master's Degree in Architecture, Urban Planning, or a related field

first_stepper.description: High school students interested in architecture, design, and urban planning can start by developing their critical thinking, writing, and communication skills.

choice_maker.description: Individuals with a generic degree in a related field (e.g., engineering, fine arts) can transition into architectural criticism by acquiring relevant skills and certifications.
certifications_or_bridge_needed: LEED AP (Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design Accredited Professional)

future_builder.description: Established architects, urban planners, or critics can advance their careers by developing expertise in specific areas, such as sustainable design, historic preservation, or urban regeneration.
future_builder.prerequisite_experience: 5+ years of experience in architecture, urban planning, or a related field

problem_solver_uturn.description: Individuals from unrelated fields can transition into architectural criticism by developing relevant skills, such as writing, research, and critical thinking.
portfolio_proof_required: Architecture or Urban Planning Project Portfolio

roadmap.title: Foundations in Architecture and Urban Planning
Developing Critical Thinking and Writing Skills
